title: Calling the Huisarts
situation: You've been having persistent migraines for three weeks. You call your huisarts (general practitioner) to schedule an appointment and ask about a possible referral to a neurologist.

phrases.hindi: U spreekt met Kavya Sharma, patiëntnummer 4872.
phrases.english: You're speaking with Kavya Sharma, patient number 4872.
phrases.context: Always open a call to the huisarts by stating your full name AND patient number. The assistant looks you up instantly in the system. Not knowing your patiëntnummer slows things down, find it on your zorgpas (insurance card) or in the patient portal.
phrases.pronunciation: u SPRAYKHT met KAV-ya SHAR-ma, pa-SHENT-num-mer VEER-duy-zend-ACHT-hon-derd-TWAY-en-ZAY-ven-tich

phrases.hindi: Ik wil graag een afspraak maken bij de huisarts.
phrases.english: I'd like to make an appointment with the GP.
phrases.context: 'Bij de huisarts' = with the GP. You make appointments with the praktijkassistent (receptionist), not the doctor directly. Be clear whether you want a 'consult' (10-min appointment) or a 'telefonisch consult' (phone consultation).
phrases.pronunciation: ik wil CHRAACH uhn AF-spraak MAA-ken bay de HUIS-arts

phrases.hindi: Ik heb al drie weken last van ernstige migraine.
phrases.english: I've been suffering from severe migraines for three weeks now.
phrases.context: 'Last hebben van' = to suffer from / to have trouble with. 'Al drie weken' = for three weeks already. 'Ernstig' = serious/severe. The duration and severity are the two key pieces of information the assistant uses for triage.
phrases.pronunciation: ik hep al DREE WAY-ken LAST van ERN-sti-che mi-CHRAY-ne

phrases.hindi: Het wordt steeds erger, de pijnaanvallen duren langer.
phrases.english: It's getting worse and worse, the pain attacks are lasting longer.
phrases.context: 'Steeds erger' = worse and worse (progressive deterioration). 'Pijnaanval' = pain attack. 'Duren' = to last. Describing the progression (not just the symptom) is important: it signals the condition is not self-resolving.
phrases.pronunciation: het WORDT STAYTS ER-cher, de PAYN-aan-val-len DUU-ren LAAN-cher

phrases.hindi: Ik heb paracetamol geprobeerd, maar dat helpt niet meer.
phrases.english: I've tried paracetamol, but that doesn't help anymore.
phrases.context: Always mention what you've already tried. 'Geprobeerd' = tried (perfect tense). 'Helpt niet meer' = no longer helps. This tells the doctor first-line treatment has failed and escalation is needed, a key triage signal.
phrases.pronunciation: ik hep pa-ra-SE-ta-mol che-pro-BAYRD, maar dat HELPT neet meer

phrases.hindi: Zou ik een verwijzing kunnen krijgen naar een neuroloog?
phrases.english: Would I be able to get a referral to a neurologist?
phrases.context: 'Verwijzing' = referral. 'Neuroloog' = neurologist. In the Dutch system you cannot self-refer to a specialist, you MUST go through the huisarts. Using 'zou ik kunnen' (would I be able to) is the politely correct way to request this.
phrases.pronunciation: zow ik uhn ver-WAY-zing KUN-nen KRAY-chen naar uhn nuh-ro-LOOCH

phrases.hindi: Soms word ik ook misselijk en gevoelig voor licht.
phrases.english: Sometimes I also become nauseous and sensitive to light.
phrases.context: Photophobia and nausea are classic migraine symptoms. 'Misselijk' = nauseous. 'Gevoelig voor licht' = sensitive to light. Mentioning associated symptoms helps the doctor classify migraine vs other headache types.
phrases.pronunciation: soms WORD ik ook MIS-se-lik en che-VOO-lich voor LICHT

phrases.hindi: Wanneer is de dokter beschikbaar en wat moet ik meenemen?
phrases.english: When is the doctor available and what should I bring?
phrases.context: 'Beschikbaar' = available. 'Meenemen' = to bring along (separable). Asking what to bring ensures you come prepared: the huisarts may want to know your current medicatie, your zorgpas, and a written symptom diary.
phrases.pronunciation: WAN-neer is de DOK-ter be-SCHIK-baar en wat MOET ik MEE-nay-men

phrases.hindi: Als het slechter wordt, moet ik dan naar de huisartsenpost?
phrases.english: If it gets worse, should I go to the out-of-hours GP centre?
phrases.context: 'Huisartsenpost' = out-of-hours GP centre (evenings, weekends, public holidays). This is NOT a hospital emergency room, it's for urgent but non-life-threatening issues when your own huisarts is closed. Always call 0900-1010 first to triage.
phrases.pronunciation: als het SLECH-ter WORDT, MOET ik dan naar de HUIS-arts-en-post

phrases.hindi: Bedankt. Dan zie ik de huisarts op donderdag om twee uur.
phrases.english: Thank you. Then I'll see the GP on Thursday at two o'clock.
phrases.context: Close the call by confirming the appointment details aloud. This prevents misunderstandings and gives the assistant a chance to correct any errors. Stating day AND time is the Dutch convention, just saying 'donderdag' isn't enough.
phrases.pronunciation: be-DANKT. dan ZEE ik de HUIS-arts op DON-der-dach om TWAY uur

grammar_notes: Duration with 'al + time expression + present tense': 'Ik heb al drie weken last van migraine' describes an ongoing state that started in the past. English uses present perfect continuous ('I have been having'); Dutch uses simple present with 'al'

Modal 'zou' for softened requests: 'Zou ik een verwijzing kunnen krijgen?' is more tentative and polite than 'Kan ik een verwijzing krijgen?'. Use 'zou' when asking for something that requires a decision from someone else
Subordinate clauses with 'omdat' (because) push the verb to the end: 'Ik bel omdat het steeds slechter wordt', 'wordt' must come last. This is a common B1 exam point

Perfect tense for completed attempts: 'Ik heb paracetamol geprobeerd' (I have tried paracetamol). The perfect is used here because the result (it didn't work) is still relevant now, classic Dutch present-perfect usage
Indirect speech with 'dat': 'De huisarts zei dat ik meer water moet drinken', the subordinate clause introduced by 'dat' again pushes the verb to the end ('drinken')
culture_notes: The huisarts is the gatekeeper to all specialist care in the Netherlands. Without a verwijzing (referral), a zorgverzekeraar (health insurer) will not cover the specialist's bill. Never try to book directly with a specialist

For evenings, weekends, and public holidays, call the Huisartsenpost (HAP) on 0900-1010. A triagist will assess urgency. For life-threatening emergencies, call 112 (ambulance). Do not go to the Spoedeisende Hulp (A&E) without being referred, you will be sent away

The recept (prescription) system: the huisarts sends prescriptions electronically to your chosen apotheek (pharmacy). You simply go to the apotheek and collect. Repeat prescriptions can often be requested via the patient portal without a new appointment

Most huisartspraktijken use a phone triage system in the morning (telefonisch spreekuur, typically 8–10am). Call early, describe your symptoms clearly and briefly, and let the assistant decide urgency. Prepared callers get appointments faster

skill_breakdown.explanation: Dutch phone calls to official services follow a fixed structure: you identify yourself first (name + patient/customer number), state the reason concisely, listen to questions, and close by confirming what was agreed. Do not start with small talk.

more_examples.hindi: U spreekt met Jan de Vries, ik bel over mijn afspraak van morgen.
more_examples.english: You're speaking with Jan de Vries, I'm calling about my appointment tomorrow.

more_examples.hindi: Kunt u mij doorverbinden met de praktijkassistent?
more_examples.english: Could you put me through to the practice assistant?

more_examples.hindi: Mag ik uw naam even herhalen voor de bevestiging?
more_examples.english: May I repeat your name for confirmation?

more_examples.hindi: Dan bevestig ik: donderdag om 14:00, huisarts De Vries.
more_examples.english: So to confirm: Thursday at 14:00, GP De Vries.

skill_breakdown.explanation: Use 'al + time + last hebben van' for ongoing conditions. Add progression ('steeds erger'), frequency ('dagelijks', 'soms'), and what hasn't helped. The triage assistant needs severity, duration, and pattern to prioritise your call.

more_examples.hindi: Ik heb al een week last van buikpijn.
more_examples.english: I've been having stomach pain for a week.

more_examples.hindi: De pijn is steeds erger geworden.
more_examples.english: The pain has been getting worse and worse.

more_examples.hindi: Het begint 's ochtends en duurt de hele dag.
more_examples.english: It starts in the morning and lasts all day.

more_examples.hindi: Ik heb ibuprofen geprobeerd maar het helpt niet.
more_examples.english: I've tried ibuprofen but it doesn't help.

more_examples.hindi: Ik heb ook koorts en ik voel me erg moe.
more_examples.english: I also have a fever and feel very tired.

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Verwijzing' (referral) is the key term. Ask 'Zou ik een verwijzing kunnen krijgen naar...?' followed by the specialist type. The huisarts may offer alternatives first, listen carefully for 'bloedonderzoek' (blood test), 'echo', or 'afwachten' (wait and see).

more_examples.hindi: Zou ik een verwijzing kunnen krijgen naar een orthopeed?
more_examples.english: Would I be able to get a referral to an orthopaedist?

more_examples.hindi: Hoe lang duurt het voordat ik terecht kan bij de specialist?
more_examples.english: How long will it take before I can see the specialist?

more_examples.hindi: Stuurt u de verwijzing naar het ziekenhuis of moet ik dat zelf doen?
more_examples.english: Will you send the referral to the hospital or do I need to do that myself?

practice_prompt: You are Marja, a praktijkassistent at Huisartsenpraktijk De Vries doing phone triage. Use formal 'u' throughout. Ask triage questions: how long, how severe (1–10), what has been tried, any other symptoms. Based on the user's answers decide: (a) urgent same-day appointment, (b) standard appointment within a week, or (c) refer to huisartsenpost. If the user asks about a verwijzing, explain that the huisarts must first assess in person before deciding. Correct grammar errors gently. Open with: 'Huisartsenpraktijk De Vries, u spreekt met Marja.'
